data.title: JBS: A Classic Cyclical Setup, But Downside Still Too Wide
data.description:
Recommendation

While JBS N.V. (JBS) offers a compelling value story, I believe the current risk-reward profile is still not attractive enough to warrant a buy. The valuation is unquestionably cheap both on absolute and relative terms. However, the timing of the cattle-cycle recovery remains uncertain, and the downside for the bear scenario remains too large. Thus, I prefer to wait for clearer evidence of margin inflection in the North American beef segment or a more favorable entry price that provides greater downside protection. This company is a hold for now.

3Q 2025 Earnings Summary

JBS delivered a slight positive Q3 earnings from robust top-line growth, improved cash generation but compressed margins. Revenue grew 13% YoY to $22.6bn, driven by broad-based strength across non-beef segments: Pilgrim's pride (+3.8% YoY), JBS Brazil (+27.7% YoY), Seara (+7.6% YoY), JBS USA Pork (+8.7% YoY), and JBS Australia (+22.9% YoY). However, consolidated margins contracted because the North America Beef segment's adjusted operating income was poor at -$95mil (-1.3% margin) despite YoY revenue growth as cattle prices hit historic levels.

Moreover, free cash flow had compressed from $994mil to $321mil YoY, partly due to higher capex. Leverage ticked up slightly to 2.39x, with net debt climbing towards $16.6 billion with an average debt tenor of 15.4 years. In short, Q3 showed that JBS is still digesting the beef-cycle pain while the other segments had done well.

Investment Thesis

JBS seems to offer an interesting and underappreciated value story due to its low valuation multiples. It is the world-leading protein producer that is trading at a fraction of the peers' valuation while at the trough of a protein cycle that could be set to rebound. The market also appears to be penalizing JBS for the current beef slump.

Beef Cycle Upswing Ahead

JBS's North America's Beef segment is currently generating losses with adjusted EBITDA at ~$2.1bn for 9M 2025 (page 40 of 6-K) due to historically low cattle supply and high input cost. In fact, the US cattle herd is at a 74-year low, partly contributed to by drought and wildfire. However, I don't think this issue would persist indefinitely, as the US cattle herds are already showing signs of rebuilding. Ranches across Nebraska, South Dakota and Texas have also begun rebuilding herds with increasing heifers' retentions, lowering beef-cow slaughter and lowering feed cost. Thus, this may lead to more cattle in the future, which would drive down cost and increase the company's margins.

But are we too early?

Even though we are already seeing more heifers' retention and cow slaughter reductions, which are signs of early rebuilding, the situation is still bad. Moreover, earnings would not normalize overnight, and it would take a while before we see material improvement. This situation is equivalent to attempting to catch a falling knife while expecting the cycle to turn from here. I think it would be prudent to wait for a few quarters first to see material improvements before adding.

Underappreciated Seara and Value-added margins

I also believe JBS's Seara prepared foods business is a hidden gem as the market undervalues it by assigning the company a low P/E multiple. Seara enjoys strong brand positions with reasonable margins, albeit lower than the previous year (current EBITDA margin ~9%) due to the avian flu and restrictions on exports to China and Europe. But these impacts are temporary. Also, this segment is more akin to a food FMCG business than a meat packer, which deserves a higher multiple.

Moreover, the margin recovery in Pilgrim's Pride from its trough in 2023 to ~11% operating margin in Q3 2025 is indeed impressive, and I think the market has not fully reflected that as well. Lower grain costs have also improved the poultry economics. Thus, the other food segments are doing better, but the market is mainly focusing on the poor beef segment narrative.

Shifting Capital Allocation

JBS is also transforming into a more shareholder-friendly entity. They are dual listed now to attract a broader investor base and unlock shareholder value. The firm also have engaged in large dividends and buybacks. That is a stark difference compared to pre-2020. The dividend per share has also increased drastically. In fact, the company has provided dividends for a decade without pausing, which is a strong testament to returning cash back to shareholders. Thus, this would bring interest to investors who feel the need for alignment with the management's capital allocation process.

Bull-Bear Case

The bull and bear cases for JBS present a wide valuation spread. In my bull case, JBS would benefit meaningfully from the US cattle-cycle recovery as slaughter numbers improve and live-cattle costs ease into 2027, which would enable the North American beef segment to swing back towards mid-cycle profitability. Combining this with margin expansion in Seara and other divisions, I estimate EPS could grow to $2.50 in 2028 from $1.9 in 2025, which is a ~11% CAGR. By assigning a 12x P/E, which is still below the sector median but at a more normalized earnings environment, the 2028 target price would be $25.

In the bear case, the cattle cycle remains weaker than expected and beef margins remain constrained. Moreover, non-beef units have also failed to offset the drag, leaving EPS closer to $1.50 by 2028. Thus, this would cause market sentiment to deteriorate further, causing market multiples to be around 5x. This would mean the 2028 target price is $7.5. That would imply a ~42% downside, which is pretty steep.

Therefore, at today's price, the risk to reward is still not favorable in my opinion. I prefer my downside to be more limited.

Valuation

JBS appears to be trading at a deep discount, which suggests to me that the market is still pricing in peak-cycle pain rather than normalized earnings level. It is trading at 6.8x forward P/E, which is a ~57% discount to its sector median (15.9x). These multiples are reserved for structurally impaired or highly leveraged companies, but it doesn't describe what JBS is today. On an enterprise basis, the disconnect is also striking. The forward EV/EBITDA of 5.0x is also ~50% discount to its sector median. Moreover, the company also offers a 10.7% dividend yield, which is >3x greater than the sector median. Although I do acknowledge that the dividend yield is too high as it already exceeds the payout ratio of 100%. Thus, I expect the dividend yield would normalize and may lower investors' sentiment.

In sum, JBS is priced as if the US beef losses are permanent. However, if looking at it from a longer-time horizon and at normalized profitability, the margins of US beef may revert to mid-cycle levels, and there would be significant re-rating. But as explained above, the downside from these levels is still too large. I'd rather wait for a lower entry price before entering.

Risk

I see the main risk is definitely the US beef cycle recovering more quickly than expected. The herd rebuilding progress may progress more smoothly, causing share price to rebound faster. JBS is also exposed to feed costs, which would impact poultry and pork margins. Sanitary risks like the avian flu or African swine fever may continue to disrupt operations. Lastly, as the company has operations outside the United States, currency swings from BRL or AUD would also provide moderate volatility to the USD-reported results. As of now, the best move, in my opinion, is to sit on the sideline and wait for a better entry price.

Conclusion

JBS is a fundamentally good and diversified protein company that is pricing in the current beef cycle pain. However, I am uncertain of the recovery timing, and the downside risks can still be quite heavy at current levels. Therefore, I believe it is prudent to stay patient and wait till we see more confirmation. Hold.

data.title: Federal Government Job Cuts Still Hitting Employment Data: The Labor Market By Major Industry In Charts
data.description:
Federal and state governments cut 122,000 employees this year through September. But other industries hired.

The headcount reduction by the federal government reached 97,000 since January, including a further reduction of 3,000 civilian employees in September, according to the nonfarm payroll report for September, released today by the Bureau of Labor Statistics.

But this does not yet include the roughly 100,000 civilian employees of the federal government that took the voluntary “deferred resignation” deal in the spring, under which they got paid through September 30. Since they were still on the payrolls in September but came off the payrolls on October 1, that reduction will hit the next nonfarm payrolls report, which will be for November, the October report having been cancelled due to the government shutdown.

State governments have shed 25,000 employees since January, including September’s increase of 16,000 (through August, they had shed 41,000 employees).

Government payrolls don’t include workers at government contractors. They’re in private-sector categories, such as in “Professional and business services,” and we have seen some declines there this year, though it is impossible to determine what portion is due to government cutbacks.

So, nonfarm payrolls without federal and state governments rose by 106,000 jobs in September from August. In June, there was zero job growth, and the trend has bounced back since then (blue columns in the chart).

Over the past three months, this measure rose by 161,000 jobs. The 3-month average, which irons out the month-to-month squiggles, rose by 54,000 jobs (red line).

Civilian employment at the federal government dropped by 3,000 in September, to 2.92 million, taking employment back to where it had been in June 2023. The share of federal employment dropped to 1.82% of total employment, the lowest in the data going back to 1939.

Total nonfarm payrolls rose by 119,000 in September from August, to 159.6 million. July and August were revised down by a combined 33,000 jobs. Over the past three months combined, 187,000 jobs were added to payrolls, even as the federal government shed 25,000 jobs over the three months, and state governments shed 12,000 jobs. The 3-month average rose to 62,000 jobs (red line).

The government shutdown in October didn’t impact the data collection for this September report – it only delayed it. But it killed the October report. October figures will be included in the November report, according to the BLS, which also explained today:

“September estimates from the establishment survey include both data collected on our normal schedule prior to the shutdown and also September data that businesses self-reported electronically during the shutdown. As a result, the establishment survey collection rate (80.2 percent) for this initial release of September 2025 data is higher than usual.”

Average hourly earnings rose by 0.25% in September from August (+3.0% annualized. The three-month average hourly earnings rose by 4.0% annualized, a slight acceleration. The three-month average has zigzagged since April.

Year-over-year, average hourly earnings rose by 3.8% in September, a slight deceleration from the prior two months, but well above the wage growth before the pandemic.

The prime-age labor force participation rate (25-to-54-year-olds) remained at 83.7% in September, the same as in August, which is historically high. The labor force participation rate shows the percentage of the population that either has a job or is looking for a job. When people retire and stop looking for a job, they exit the labor force but remain in the population until they die.

The surge of boomer retirements, which started about 15 years ago and continues, has pushed down the overall labor force participation rate, as these retired boomers are still in the population but no longer in the labor force. The prime-age labor force participation rate eliminates the issue of the retiring boomers.

The unemployment rate (U-3) rose to 4.4% in September, still historically low, but quite a bit higher than it had been in recent years. This is in line with the Fed’s projections. In its last “Summary of Economic Projections” (SEP), released at the September meeting, the Fed projected the unemployment rate to reach 4.5% by the end of 2025.

This rising unemployment rate has been among the causes for the Fed’s labor market concerns and rate cuts, despite accelerating inflation and “solid” economic growth through Q3.

The unemployment rate reflects the number of unemployed people who are actively looking for a job divided by the labor force (people working or looking for a job).

Jobs by Major Industry over time

The jobs in each industry are defined by work location. The primary activity at that facility determines the industry category. For example, a worker at an Amazon fulfillment center would be under “transportation and warehousing,” not “retail.” It’s not the company that matters, but the work being done at that specific location.

Healthcare and social assistance:

  • Total employment: 23.5 million.
  • 3-month average growth: +56,000

Professional and business services: includes facilities whose employees work primarily in Professional, Scientific, and Technical Services; Management of Companies and Enterprises; Administrative and Support, and Waste Management and Remediation Services.

It includes jobs in tech and social media. The increasing use of AI, which started years ago, has also been figured as one of the reasons for the reductions in employment.

The explosion of employment in 2021 and 2022 turned out to be over-hiring. Companies then came to their senses and began shedding the excess they’d added over those years.

  • Total employment: 22.5 million
  • 3-month average change: -17,000

Leisure and hospitality - restaurants, lodging, resorts, etc. After the collapse of employment during the lockdowns, the industry’s employment has fully recovered and has continued to grow:

  • Total employment: 17.1 million, new record.
  • 3-month average growth: +29,000

Retail trade includes workers at brick-and-mortar retail stores, such as malls, auto dealers, grocery stores, gas stations, etc., and other retail locations such as markets. It does not include the tech-related jobs of ecommerce operations, drivers, and warehouse employees.

A big portion of this sector has been under pressure from ecommerce, and dozens of major retailers have been liquidated in bankruptcy court, some of which we have documented since 2016 in our Brick-and-Mortar Meltdown series. The long-term decline in employment began in 2017, driven by employment at malls, with malls themselves turning into zombie malls around the country.

The brick-and-mortar retailers that are doing well are those that are selling groceries, motor vehicles, gasoline, and other products that are under less pressure from ecommerce.

  • Total employment: 15.6 million
  • 3-month average growth: +8,000.

Manufacturing: Automation is key and drives manufacturing in the US. Companies are investing lots of money to push automation to the next level. Automation replaces lots of human labor doing repetitive manual work. Manufacturing jobs of today often require high skill levels and include tech jobs.

  • Total employment: 12.7 million
  • 3-month average growth: -10,000

Financial activities (finance and insurance plus real estate renting, leasing, buying, selling, and management). With home sales down by 25% for three years, and mortgage originations down by even more, a large number of real estate agents have exited, as have employees at mortgage lenders and mortgage brokers. We can see that here.

  • Total employment: 9.2 million, new record
  • 3-month average change: -3,000

Construction (all types, from single-family housing to highways). Construction in the housing sector and the office has been hit by a lack of demand. But construction in other sectors has been stronger, and construction of data centers, factories, and power plants has been booming amid complaints of shortages of skilled labor.

  • Total employment: 8.31 million
  • 3-month average growth: +1,000

Transportation and Warehousing:

  • Total employment: 6.7 million
  • 3-month average growth: +1,000

Wholesale Trade:

  • Total employment: 6.2 million.
  • 3-month average growth: -1,000

“Other services” includes equipment and machinery repairing, promoting or administering religious activities, grantmaking, advocacy, dry cleaning, laundry services, personal care services, death care services, pet care services, photofinishing services, temporary parking services, and dating services.

  • Total employment: 6.05 million
  • 3-month average growth: +7,000

“Information” includes facilities where people primarily work on web search portals, data processing, data transmission, information services, software publishing, motion picture and sound recording, broadcasting including over the Internet, and telecommunications.

This includes work sites by tech and social media companies that had gone on an over-hiring binge during the pandemic, and they have admitted it, and they have reversed course.

And AI has had a profound impact in these sectors for years.

  • Total employment: 2.93 million
  • 3-month average growth: -4,000

Arts, Entertainment, and Recreation:

  • Total employment: 2.73 million, a record
  • 3-month average growth: +8,000

Federal government:

  • Total employment: 2.92 million
  • 3-month average growth: -8,000

State governments: A large part of employment here is in colleges and universities, and in healthcare. First responders and law enforcement are also large components.

  • Total employment: 5.5 million
  • 3-month average growth: -4,000

Local governments: Most of the jobs are in education across all grade levels, from preschool to community colleges and trade schools, in healthcare, and law enforcement.

  • Total employment: 15.2 million
  • 3-month average growth: +18,000

data.title: AMG GW&K Small Cap Core Fund Q3 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Performance Drivers

  • The stock market soared in the third quarter, with gold mining, uranium, quantum computing, artificial intelligence (AI), and meme stocks driving impressive, concentrated gains. However, while the Russell 2000® Index reached new highs, most small caps lagged the benchmark.
  • The GW&K Small Cap Core Fund (GWEIX) posted a positive return, but underperformed its benchmark, missing those speculative rallies in mining, technology, and meme stocks due to its focus on consistent earnings.
  • What made the relative performance all the more frustrating is that our portfolio companies generally performed quite well on their reported second-quarter results and forward guidance. Thematic risk and speculative stocks ripped higher, while the remainder stood by and watched, preparing to tip their hats.

Top Contributors/Detractors

  • Current earnings and cash flow were not the main focus for investors in the quarter. This in large measure drove the meaningful underperformance for our Fund, where the emphasis is on the likelihood of consistent earnings and cash flow growth.

Contributors

  • The health care sector had good stock selection; our best actor here was Medpace (MEDP).

Detractors

  • The worst performing sector relatively was information technology, which came up nearly 300 bps short of the benchmark. Our largest holding here, MACOM Technology (MTSI), finished the quarter down 13%. MACOM produced a fine quarter, however, investors favored Credo Technology (CRDO), Rigetti Computing (RGTI), and Rambus (RMBS) — which combined to produce half of the industry return.

Positioning Update

  • During the quarter, we added six new holdings and eliminated three. We added two stocks in biotech as we eliminated one due to market cap appreciation.
  • We also added two stocks in the technology sector to beef up our exposure to AI infrastructure and enterprise network security.
  • We added a new defense company and swapped one insurance company for another in an attempt to upgrade quality in that industry. Finally, one name was eliminated due to a pending takeover.

Outlook

  • We are finding companies and stocks now that fit our process and adding them to portfolios. Whether they will appreciate more quickly than other benchmark stocks in the immediate term is unknown. The short run is hard to predict consistently, but in the long run, we tip our hats to earnings and cash flow.
  • Looking ahead, our team seeks companies with strong market positions and management, prioritizing long-term earnings and cash flow growth while remaining cautious about short-term market trends.

data.title: Delek Logistics Partners: Double-Digit Yield And Significant Catalysts Advancing
data.description:
Introduction & Financials

Delek Logistics Partners, LP (DKL) is the midstream company formed by Delek US Holdings (DK) to own and operate crude oil and refined products logistics and marketing assets, recently taking aggressive expansion steps, including a couple of acquisitions and water solutions expansion, and currently paying a very attractive nearly 10% yield.

About 80% of Delek Logistics used to be owned by Delek US Holdings, which was reduced quite a bit in recent years to ~63.3% as of Q3 2025, which is part of a broader plan to reduce this concentration and allow DKL to optimize and create its own value rather than depending on DK.

The company is diversifying away from Delek Holdings, aiming to "de-risk" by expanding and going into the water disposal business for the oil and gas industry with the acquisition of H20 Midstream in late 2024 for ~$230 million (~$160 million cash, ~$70 million convertible preferred equity) and Gravity for $300.8 million at the beginning of 2025 ($209.3 million in cash and $91.5 million in stock), and participating in a handful of pipeline joint ventures.

As we can see, the company’s Distributable Cash Flow continued to grow, with the Q3 2025 Adjusted DCF coming in at $74.1 million, being on track for yet another record with $221.74 million YTD. On a per share level, the Q3’25 Adjusted DCF came in at $1.38 compared to $1.31 in Q3’24, for a solid ~5.4% increase YoY while the company is still expanding significantly. Annualized, we’d look at about $295.65 million this year, which is very solid for a $2.43 billion market cap.

They have the advantage of being exposed to Delaware’s natural gas production growth, which is, as they say, unmatched in rock quality, remaining inventory, and well productivity, with the remaining inventory currently being restricted because of the lack of sour gas handling capabilities, so it’s a long-term play that can benefit them quite a bit at a key time for natural gas and LNG.

Despite commissioning their Libby 2 processing plant in the Northern Delaware Basin that has sour gas treating capability a few months ago (expected to be accretive to 2026 DCF/share, they saw the market shift to a point where they’ll need to expand the processing capacity earlier than expected around sour gas, as mentioned during the Q3 Earnings Release:

Originally, and based on producers' forecast when we started Libby 2, we anticipated to fill the plant with sweet gas, but the landscape has changed and producer needs solution and rapid solution for sour gas. As a result, we accelerated our sour programs to provide solution in a more rapid time line. We have very, very high confidence in not only filling up Libby 2 with sour gas, but because of the full sweet, sour gas, crude and water solution that we provide, we will need to expand processing capacity earlier than our previous expectation around sour.

Financially, based on Delek Logistics' latest report, we can see the current assets basically matching the current liabilities, but a ton of debt on the balance sheet as their expansion doesn’t come for free. Still, there’s no debt until 2028, and the company issued 7.375% 2033 notes this year, with the new weighted average interest rate being 7.39% now, which is not the best, but it’s normal for a company like this in this environment.

They’ll benefit from rate cuts as they advance, and the fact that they don’t need to refinance for a couple of years means that they can get better yields in the future if they wait. Sure, they can continue to refinance these and even benefit from a lower-rate environment, but it’s a risk to keep in mind and ultimately something they should consider repaying if they can’t find better yields in their investments.

Shareholder Returns

Delek Logistics pays a very solid ~9.94% yield that represents about $178.83 million YTD and should come to roughly $240 million per year, for a payout ratio of roughly 80.6% YTD and ~81.2% for the whole year, which is definitely attractive.

Not only that, but back in February 2025, they also announced a buyback program for up to $150 million until the end of 2026, for a total potential buyback yield of ~6.17% over these two years. This offsets the recent issuing to some degree, but the company will ultimately rely on issuing stock and debt to develop, so keep in mind that there’s likely more dilution ahead. Again, it’s not really dilution since it’s used to develop (see the recent acquisitions that are a mix of cash and stock), and it can ultimately lead to accretive per share growth, so I can’t say I mind it.

Risks and Opportunities

The key near-term catalyst for Delek Logistics seems to be rate cuts, which can potentially help the company directly by lowering their interest expenses ($342.7 million TTM, which is massive), potentially unlocking a lot of growth ahead as they refinance and win from this spread, while also directly helping their clients and partners in similar ways. For a company like DKL, we’re talking about a significant factor of relief, as a big chunk of their debt came after the hikes, meaning that refinancing them in the near future with lower rates can save significant amounts of cash - which would directly benefit the shareholders.

This also leads to lower treasury yields in time, assuming that nothing fundamental changes that would force the US to borrow under worse conditions, which can make the cost of ignoring Delek Logistics' very attractive yield and solid payout ratio too much for the market to afford.

But, as I mentioned before, rate cuts won’t instantly fix everything. Even here we can notice how it will likely take several years to see this being reflected in their valuation (if it does) and interest cost structure through refinancing. The near-term future of these cuts is in a risky spot right now, with the US Government recently going through what became its longest shutdown in history, which also caused the BLS to halt its data collection and publishing (with a new release only in November). This causes the Fed to have less up-to-date data for its next meeting at a time when we’re already seeing a lot of uncertainty around tariffs, inflation, and unemployment, which Powell even mentioned during the October meeting.

Another risk is that despite the recent expansion and diversification steps, they’ll continue to depend on Delek Holdings to some degree, at least for now. As their main shareholder, Delek Holdings will likely continue to have its best interests in mind, which can sometimes differ from Delek Logistics'. Still, both of them can benefit from policies like the small refinery exemption, while midstream and water are key parts of the oil and gas industry and their future.

Of course, don’t expect things to be perfectly smooth with a company that ultimately depends on the oil and gas industry. Although Delek Logistics' DCF has been very solid despite the volatility in oil and gas prices, which don’t affect them directly, we’re still talking about a midstream company, and when the up or downstream (or both) have issues, this can affect the whole supply chain. Especially as the company is expanding into water solutions, their exposure to the performance of these companies grows even more.

When their clients have issues and are forced to cut costs, this can eventually spread into Delek Logistics' numbers, so even though they don’t get affected by these fluctuations directly, weakness in the industry can still hurt them.

Still, a general thing that the market will definitely pay very close attention to is the US-Venezuela situation, with the most recent move (after the US placed an aircraft carrier in the Caribbean and striking over 20 ships there) being to prepare a plan to designate a Venezuelan drug cartel as a foreign terrorist organization. Trump hinted that this move could allow the US to target certain assets and infrastructure in the country, despite saying he’s not planning an attack on the country a few weeks ago but also saying Maduro’s days in office are numbered.

Why this is important is because Venezuela is not a random target. The country has the largest proven reserves of oil on the planet, roughly 4 times what the US or Russia have. However, being one of the founding members of OPEC, it’s unlikely to see its allies just sit and let the US strike whatever they want in the country, which is why the geopolitical magnitude of anything in Venezuela can have massive effects on the price of oil and the market’s sentiment. Russia said it’s “ready” to help Venezuela’s military, with the latter rallying troops recently and Maduro even telling Trump to avoid a “forever war” in the region similar to the one in Afghanistan.

So, Delek Logistics' management will have to continue navigating an uncertain environment, and they definitely have to be careful with their debt and leverage, as that has been rapidly getting to quite a significant level.

Peer Comparison

Compared to a wide set of peers including Global Partners LP (GLP), Sunoco LP (SUN), Genesis Energy, L.P. (GEL), Martin Midstream Partners L.P. (MMLP), MPLX LP (MPLX), Hess Midstream LP (HESM), and Plains All American Pipeline, L.P. (PAA), we see that they aren’t the best in any regard, but note that the PB is affected by Delek Holdings' equity, while they’re paying the best yield in the entire group. They aren’t really that cheap, but it’s also worth mentioning that their profitability is strong and the company is expanding quite a bit, so it makes sense to see them trading at a premium.

Valuation

For Delek Logistics' valuation, let’s start with the estimated $295 million in Adjusted DCF this year. Let's assume that they grow at a 5% CAGR for the first 5 years thanks to their growth moves, Libby 2 cash flow accretion, and rate cuts lowering interest expenses, followed by a 4% CAGR for the subsequent 5 years and a 2.5% terminal growth rate. I’ll go for a WACC of 8.5% (above my estimated ~7.24%; assuming a ~7.1% cost of equity and ~6.9% cost of debt) for a better margin of safety, with $2.29 billion in net debt and 53.52 million shares outstanding as of the Q3 report. This gets us to an enterprise value of $5.71 billion, an equity value of $3.42 billion, and an intrinsic value of $63.96 per share, which is much better than the current level despite the relatively conservative assumptions.

Conclusions

I rate Delek Logistics a Strong Buy, as their key assets, long-term potential, expansion moves, and very attractive plus sustainable yield with buybacks on top make the potential returns very solid. The risk remains, especially since they have relatively high debt and depend on a volatile market, but the fundamentals are still solid, the debt is not due for a couple more years, and they are expanding, all while paying a strong yield that can make waiting for improvements worth it.

data.title: Unit Corporation: Drilling Division Sale Gives It Plenty Of Cash But Reduces Its Free Cash Flow
data.description:
Unit Corporation (OTCQX:UNTC) sold its drilling division for $119.7 million in cash at the start of October. I now project it to end 2025 with $180 million in cash if it doesn't declare a special dividend.

Unit's challenge now is that it probably won't generate enough cash flow from its remaining upstream division to cover its $1.25 per share quarterly dividend. I currently project its 2026 dividend coverage at approximately 0.6x, although it could draw down its cash position over multiple years to help pay for its dividend.

I previously estimated Unit's value at $31 per share (at the end of 2025) and believe that it is still a reasonable estimate.

After the drilling division sale and updated data, I have a valuation range of $30.70 to $35.65 per share for Unit. This assumes that it wins the warrant litigation battle. A full loss of the warrant litigation may reduce its value to around $28.25 to $32.45 per share instead.

Sale of Drilling Division

At the start of October, Unit sold its contract drilling subsidiary to Cactus Drilling Company for $119.7 million in cash consideration.

I had previously valued Unit's drilling division at $13 per share (or approximately $128 million), so this is close to my estimate.

This leaves Unit with its upstream division as its sole remaining business segment.

Q3 2025 Upstream Results

Unit reported total production averaging 10,511 BOEPD in Q3 2025, including approximately 2,207 barrels per day of oil production. This was flat quarter-over-quarter oil production growth, while Unit's total production declined by 6.5% compared to Q2 2025. This was driven by a high-single digit quarter-over-quarter decline in Unit's production of both natural gas and NGLs. Unit spent $4.7 million on oil and gas capex in Q3 2025.

Potential 2026 Outlook

I've modeled out a scenario where Unit continues to operate as an upstream company in 2026. This scenario involves it keeping its production levels roughly flat compared to Q3 2025 levels.

At the current 2026 strip of $58 to $59 WTI oil and $4.15 NYMEX natural gas, Unit is projected to generate $109 million in revenues after hedges. This includes $7 million in interest income, which assumes that Unit doesn't pay out a special dividend that reduces its cash on hand.

I've modeled Unit's cash G&A at around $14 million per year after divesting its contract drilling business, down from around $20 million currently. I've also allowed for $20 million in capex to maintain production levels.

This result in a projection that Unit can generate $31 million in free cash flow in 2026 before dividends if it wants to maintain production levels.

Unit's current $1.25 per share quarterly dividend adds up to around $49 million per year in dividend payments, so its cash total would go down by around $18 million if it maintained that dividend.

If Unit paid out a special dividend (of $10 per share) in late 2025, this would also reduce its interest income by around $4 million from what I've modeled here.

Cash On Hand and Dividends

Unit had $63 million in cash on hand at the end of Q3 2025. If it maintains its $1.25 per share quarterly dividend and doesn't pay a special dividend, then I'd expect it to end 2025 with roughly $180 million in cash on hand. This also assumes that there are no cash taxes from the sale of its drilling division.

Thus, if Unit doesn't pay a special dividend, it should be able to maintain a $1.25 per share quarterly dividend in 2026 (and potentially for a considerable period after that while drawing down on its cash position). Unit would finish 2026 with around $162 million in cash on hand in this scenario.

If Unit pays a $10 per share special dividend in late 2025 instead, that would leave it with around $81 million in cash on hand at the end of 2025. Unit's 2026 cash burn after its $1.25 per share quarterly dividend would increase to $22 million due to lower interest income, leaving it with $59 million in cash on hand at the end of 2026. That would put it more at risk of a dividend cut in future years.

Upstream Asset Valuation

The main remaining unknown is around what Unit's upstream assets are worth. Amplify Energy's Oklahoma assets recently sold for 0.5x PD PV-10 (based on $65 oil and $4 natural gas).

Unit reported reserves with a PD PV-10 (at the end of 2024) of $245 million at $67.90 oil and $3.77 natural gas. The valuation at $65 oil and $4 natural gas would be essentially the same.

Thus Unit's upstream value would be around $123 million based on that comparison at a 0.5x multiple.

Unit's assets have significantly higher current production compared to total reserves (relative to Amplify's Oklahoma assets), so perhaps they may receive a better price due to the near-term cash flow generation potential.

At a 0.7x multiple instead, Unit's upstream assets would be worth around $172 million. This would be around 3.0x Unit's unhedged 2026 EBITDA at current strip, excluding G&A.

Notes on Valuation

As noted above, Unit is projected to end 2025 with $180 million in cash on hand if it doesn't pay out a special dividend.

Unit's upstream division is valued at $123 million to $172 million. This results in a combined estimated value of $30.70 to $35.65 per share at the end of 2025.

This does not include any impact from the warrant litigation, and if the warrants are fully adjusted for past dividends, that would reduce Unit's value to $28.25 to $32.45 per share.

These numbers are similar to the prior $31 per share valuation that I had estimated for Unit.

Conclusion

Unit sold its contract drilling division for approximately $120 million in cash and should end 2025 with around $180 million in cash before any special dividend outlays.

Unit could continue to pay its $1.25 per share quarterly dividend but would either need to start using its cash position to support its dividend or trade off production declines for improved near-term free cash flow.

I now estimate Unit's value at around $30.70 to $35.65 per share (at the end of 2025), excluding any impact from warrant litigation. If Unit loses the case, its value could be reduced to $28.25 to $32.45 per share instead.

data.title: VBR: $58B Small-Cap Value ETF Delivering Above Average Returns
data.description:
Investment Thesis

The Vanguard Small-Cap Value ETF (VBR) has attracted over $58B in assets under management and has delivered above-average returns over the last five and ten years. Undoubtedly, it's a solid, low-cost, and well-diversified option for investors looking to move away from the large caps. However, I'm not convinced it's the best option, so today, I will compare VBR with a few value ETFs you may not have considered before. They're a little more costly, but two stand out to me for their superior fundamentals, which is why I've decided to limit my rating on VBR to a "hold." I hope you enjoy the read.

Vanguard Small-Cap Value ETF Overview

Strategy and Discussion

VBR tracks the CRSP US Small Cap Value Index, selecting companies ranked in the bottom 2-15% by total market capitalization. The Index ranks stocks using a multi-factor model that assigns stocks to either a growth or value style. For value, statistics include book-to-price, estimated earnings-to-price, historical earnings-to-price, dividend-to-price, and sales-to-price ratios. These are pretty standard valuation metrics, but one obvious advantage is that they're rules-based and multiple ratios are used. I also appreciate that historical and estimated ratios are used, though my preference for this size segment is that estimated ratios are more emphasized. After all, these are smaller companies that, ideally, are growing quickly, so historical metrics might not be as relevant.

Importantly, VBR isn't quite a small-cap ETF. Per its Index page, there were 823 companies in the Index as of September 30, 2025, with market caps between $340M and $31.3B. Furthermore, since the Index is free-float market-cap-weighted, these larger companies have an outsized influence, and based on my calculations, VBR's weighted market cap is about $9.5B. That's about twice the size of the iShares S&P Small-Cap 600 Value ETF (IJS) and three times the size of the iShares Russell 2000 Value ETF (IWN).

Therefore, VBR might be better classified as a small/mid-cap value ETF, a consequence of CRSP's unique method that allows some overlap between its size segments. For practical purposes, I don't think it matters too much if you know what you own and that it doesn't overlap with your existing holdings (please feel free to check overlap here). However, it might expand the number of ETFs you might be considering into the mid-cap category.

Fund Facts, Characteristics, and QDI Estimates

Before we get to the analysis, here's a quick rundown of various fund facts and characteristics, pulled from VBR's website, fact sheet, latest annual financial statements, and its quote page on Seeking Alpha.

  • Current Price: $201.57
  • Exchange: NYSE Arca
  • Assets Under Management: $58.35B
  • Shares Outstanding: 150,633,067
  • 50-Day Average Volume: 310,797
  • Expense Ratio: 0.07%
  • 30 Day Median Bid/Ask Spread: 0.04%
  • Inception Date: January 26, 2004
  • Current Index: CRSP US Small Cap Value Index
  • Previous Index: (MSCI US Small Cap Value Index through April 16, 2013)
  • Trailing Dividend Yield: 2.07%
  • Five-Year Dividend CAGR: 10.54%
  • Dividend Schedule: Quarterly
  • Top Sectors: Industrials (22.6%) and Financials (19.5%)
  • Assets In Top 10: 6.3%
  • Holdings: 843
  • Portfolio Turnover: 15.8%

These statistics indicate VBR is highly liquid, well-established, cost-efficient, and well-diversified, at least at the company level. At the sector level, it's slightly concentrated in Industrials and Financials, but overall, it has decent exposure to most sectors as of October 31, 2025.

Worth noting is the 8.3% allocation to Real Estate, and along with about 1.4% allocated to Mortgage REITs in the Financials sector, this means not all distributions are classified as Qualified Dividend Income. While REITs account for about 10% of the portfolio by weight, my calculations show they actually contribute about 26% of the fund's yield, which is why Vanguard's Tax Center estimates about 74% QDI for 2025. VBR's 17 Mortgage REITs alone yield 12.6% on average, contributing 8% of the 26% in non-QDI.

Vanguard Small-Cap Value ETF Performance

VBR Ten-Year Total Returns vs. Peer Group

First, I want to highlight VBR's ten-year returns compared to other small/mid-cap value peers, but rather than using an ETF's official category, I've narrowed the list by their weighted average market caps instead, setting $12B as the maximum. That should capture a few mid-cap value ETFs that might also be good comparators (RFV, EZM, IVOV, IJJ, MDYV), and surprisingly, 26 funds met that criteria. I've listed them all in the table below, and you'll notice VBR ranks #9/26, with a 145.78% ten-year total return through October 2025.

VBR Five-Year Total Returns vs. Peer Group

Over the last five years, VBR's 100.88% total return ranks #12/36 among U.S. value ETFs with market caps below $12B, so again, this is a solid, above-average result. However, we still see a few "pure value" ETFs outperforming, plus a few relatively new funds like the Avantis U.S. Small Cap Value Fund (AVUV) and the Dimensional US Small Cap Value ETF (DFSV) doing well. With market caps of $3.3B and $4.8B, respectively, they're truly "small-cap" ETFs, so I'd like to add them to the comparisons list to see if they might be better picks than VBR.

Vanguard Small-Cap Value ETF Analysis

The following table highlights selected fundamental metrics for VBR's top ten holdings, which comprise just 6.38% of the fund. This low concentration level is a testament to its strong diversification features, but unfortunately, it makes analysis slightly more difficult. That's why I'd like to focus on the summary metrics at the bottom, so please have a scan of them to see how they compare against the peers I selected, and I'll offer my takeaways afterward.

VBR: Below Average Size and Value Factor Exposure

With a $9.5B weighted average market cap and 12.97x forward P/E ratio, VBR isn't exactly the best "small-cap value" fund of the group. RFV and RZV offer the cheapest valuations, and you might be surprised that RFV's market cap is quite a bit lower than VBR despite being an S&P MidCap 400 ETF. That's why names can be deceiving, and these are probably two ETFs you might not have considered before despite their strong historical returns.

AVUV, DFSV, and EZM also feature cheaper forward P/E and trailing P/CF ratios than VBR, and the only ETF that VBR appears to outperform on valuation is IJJ. Therefore, while all funds listed are, indeed, cheap at 10-13x forward earnings, VBR definitely doesn't stand out.

VBR: Positive Historical and Estimated Earnings Growth

RFV and RZV might have good five- and ten-year track records, but I find it alarming that their constituents have seen their earnings per share decrease by 10.45% and 7.68% per year over the last three years. That's likely the reason for their cheap valuations and "pure value" status, and in my view, a couple extra points of savings on the P/E ratio isn't worth the gamble that its holdings are, in fact, due for a turnaround. These ETFs have a history of large drawdowns, especially in Q1 2020, when they declined by 44.85% and 55.62%, respectively, compared to 35.28% for VBR.

On the other hand, VBR's holdings have increased earnings by 0.47% per year over the last three years and have forecasted growth of 7.42% over the next twelve months. Relatively speaking, these are good figures for the category, and although EZM looks slightly better, I'm relieved to see VBR offers at least some balance between value and growth.

VBR: Relatively High Dividend Yield and Quality Features

VBR's holdings yield 2.13% on a weighted average basis, and after subtracting the fund's 0.07% expense ratio, shareholders should net 2.06%. Performing the same calculation for its peers results in the following:

  • RFV: 2.34% - 0.35% = 1.99%
  • EZM: 1.77% - 0.38% = 1.39%
  • IJJ: 2.09% - 0.18% = 1.91%
  • RZV: 1.80% - 0.35% = 1.45%
  • AVUV: 1.93% - 0.25% = 1.68%
  • DFSV: 2.02% - 0.30% = 1.72%

To reiterate an earlier point, only about 74% of VBR's dividends will count as QDI in 2025, but it's still a reasonably high-yielding fund for the category. Furthermore, VBR has good quality features, including 10.74% net margins and an 11.67% weighted average ROE. EZM, IJJ, and AVUV also look strong, whereas RFV and RZV's low margins provide investors with yet another reason why their holdings are trading at such cheap valuations.

Investment Recommendation

VBR is a solid small/mid-cap value fund with a 2.06% estimated yield, a 13x forward P/E ratio, positive historical and estimated earnings growth, and double-digit profit margins. Along with strong diversification and a 0.07% expense ratio, these statistics demonstrate why VBR has over $58B in assets under management and has been one of the top performers in its category over the last five and ten years. However, I'm leaning toward EZM for its superior growth and quality characteristics, as well as AVUV, for those looking for a "true" small-cap value fund. Given these options, I think the best rating for VBR is a "hold," but I still hope you found this analysis helpful, and as always, I look forward to your comments below. Thank you for reading.

Investment Philosophy

Purchase durable business franchises that are selling at a discount to their intrinsic value.

Investment Process

We utilize a business owner's approach to investing, thor- oughly investigating the economics of the business and the quality of the management team. Some of the character- istics of good businesses include strong recurring revenue and attractive returns-on-invested capital (ROIC). We have a strong orientation to low absolute and relative valuation, which are key to the execution of our investment strategy. A new idea will come from a variety of sources including company visits, screens, conferences, trade periodicals and general reading. All members of the research team are re- sponsible for fundamental research.

Once an investment opportunity is identified it is put through an extensive due diligence process, which typical- ly includes management interviews and site visits. When an acceptable level of conviction is achieved, the appropri- ate weighting (considering liquidity, valuation, etc.) is dis- cussed and determined. A new company purchased in the portfolio will usually have an initial position size of 2-4%. The portfolio generally consists of 25-40 companies and is diversified across industries.

We are long-term investors, a typical holding period for our companies is three to five years, and portfolio turnover averages 20-40% per annum. Since inception, the strategy has generally hedged a significant portion of its currency.

Safran's profits are primarily driven by the aftermarket. We initiated a position in the company in 2018 and held the company through the COVID downturn. data.title: Advance Auto Parts: Turnaround Is Progressing (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
Shares of Advance Auto Parts (AAP) are up 25% over the past year, though performance has been very volatile as investors gauge what has been a choppy turnaround effort. Indeed, shares are down over 20% from their recent high as concerns have mounted about discretionary consumer spending and potential cost pressures from tariffs that may be difficult to pass on. These concerns led me to rate shares a sell in August, and since then, the stock has lost 14%, and shares are now trading around my $48-50 fair value. Given this and with updated financials, now is a good time to revisit Advance Auto Parts.

Margin improvement has been impressive

In the company’s third quarter, reported on October 30th, Advance Auto Parts earned $0.92, which beat estimates by $0.17 even as revenue fell by 5% to $2 billion. This decline in revenue is really due to the impact of store closures earlier this year. Same-store sales were actually up 3% from last year. Adjusted results exclude $57 million of one-time costs in the quarter, primarily related to ongoing efforts to restructure and improve the business. AAP generated a 4.4% operating margin in Q3, up 370 bps from last year.

Frankly, there were encouraging signs in this quarter as operational improvements took hold. Adjusted gross margins expanded 260 bps to 44.8%, despite the pressure of tariffs. It seems AAP has been able to pass on much of this cost to consumers. On the earnings call, management said that AAP raised prices about 3% from last year, and the number of transactions was down slightly. This was up slightly from the 2% inflation in Q2 as tariff costs continue to work their way through the supply chain. Management expects a further sequential increase in prices in Q4 to 4%, with a further increase in Q1 of next year, at which point it should begin leveling out.

These price increases are primarily to offset costs rather than intended to expand margins. Competition remains elevated, reducing the capacity to raise prices too much, especially in a mixed consumer environment. Management sees positive sales in the Pro channel while do-it-yourself activity is choppier. Pro same-store sales were up 4%. In contrast, AAP is seeing pressure from low and mid-income households as they more carefully watch budgets amid persistent inflation pressures.

With the cost of routine jobs increasing, management sees a risk that some consumers are taking longer to do regular maintenance work (i.e. extending an oil change to 8,000 miles from 7,500). Ultimately, I see the DIY channel continuing to underperform the pro channel. That said, a recession is not my base case, and a likely larger tax refund season in Q1 (given changes to tax law this July) could help demand recover. I do not see consumer spending surging by any means, but these factors mitigate the risk of decline.

In-store improvements take hold

Importantly, AAP’s self-help measures are gaining traction. Product availability in stores has improved about 500 bps and is running in the 96-97% area. AAP is now approaching its high 90% availability target. Investments in supply chain management and SKU optimization are paying off, and having in-stock availability is essential to maintain customer relationships as few things are more frustrating than going to a store only for the desired product to be out of stock. Along these lines, AAP has also invested in improving the in-store experience by enhancing staffing. The time-to-serve customers has improved by over 10 minutes but is still a bit above the 30-40 minute target.

Even with these changes, SG&A of $823 million was 40.4% of sales. This was down 110bps from last year given stronger sales at stores that remained open. SG&A spending was down nearly 8% from last year. This expense performance has been very encouraging, given the fact that Advance Auto Parts has also been able to improve the customer experience at the same time.

Cash generation is muted

AAP also has its balance sheet in a reasonable position. Net leverage ended the quarter at 2.6x. The company ended the quarter with $3.2 billion of cash, $3.4 billion of debt and $1.9 billion of operating leases. Cash is elevated given ongoing restructuring costs. There have been $130 million of cash costs associated with its restructuring efforts this year, and I see ~$500-$800 million of cash needs over the next two years. This includes its 2% dividend yield. Its dividend costs about $60 million per year.

That said, inventories are up $72 million this year to $3.7 billion, even with sales running lower. This reflects the need to improve inventory availability after struggling with low in-stock levels in prior years. Free cash flow is -$277 million so far this year as working capital has been a drag, which is consistent with the typical seasonal pattern. In fact, working capital has been a $460 million headwind this year.

As you can see below, it raised the low end of its sales expectations while moving its operating margin and EPS outlooks closer to the prior midpoints. Even as it cut its cap-ex spending, free cash flow is likely to be a bit worse than previously expected, pointing to somewhat weaker working capital recovery in Q4.

Shares are fairly valued

Back in August, I was targeting $4.50-$5.00 in 2027 earnings. Management is targeting a 7% operating margin over the medium term, which could support over $6 in EPS, but I remain cautious about this occurring. Much of the supply chain savings has already occurred, and I am cautious about the ability to move prices much higher without sacrificing transaction volumes. Still, the company clearly has momentum, and margins should get past 5%.

AAP is delivering on its own operational goals, significantly improving the in-store experience, which should help it gain market share. That said, the underlying macro environment is mixed at best, and this is likely to limit potential sales growth. Plus, this company is unlikely to generate enough cash flow to cover its dividend until 2027 at the soonest. In other words, the turnaround is clearly gaining traction, but it will be a long road to complete the turnaround.

I still view ~$4.50-$5.00 as the company’s steady-state earnings power, which should be achievable in two years. At 10x 2027 earnings, with capital returns beyond the current dividend unlikely for some time, I struggle to see scope for much further multiple expansion. That said, shares are now more appropriately factoring in the length of the turnaround. As such, I am upgrading the stock to a “hold.” I would rather see shares at the lower-$40s before actively buying, but there is no pressing need to sell after recent underperformance.

data.title: After The Renaissance: Crypto Meets Its Reckoning
data.description:
When we wrote nearly a year ago about crypto’s “renaissance” in the wake of President Donald Trump’s return to the White House, Bitcoin (BTC-USD) had just surged past $95,000 and the digital asset complex was basking in glory with a total market cap exceeding over $3 trillion.

Deregulation was back in vogue, the SEC had finally approved spot Bitcoin ETFs, and the Trump campaign’s embrace of crypto gave believers something they had always craved: political capital at the highest echelons of the U.S. government.

Yet the reality of the past twelve months has proven more complicated. The same political environment that once set the stage for Bitcoin’s rise has now become a sobering lens through which we evaluate the structural weaknesses that never fully disappeared. While crypto enjoyed its moment in the sun, the subsequent correction has forced a reconsideration of what the past year has truly achieved.

Euphoria Peaks and the Market Turns

Bitcoin’s dramatic surge above $125,000 early in the autumn was fueled by growing acceptance across Wall Street, record inflows into spot Bitcoin ETFs, and a continued belief that the administration was preparing to treat Bitcoin as a strategic national asset. The optimism felt powerful and at times rather eerily reminiscent of earlier boom cycles observed in 2017 and 2021.

The CoinDesk 20 Index, which measures the performance of the 20 largest and most liquid digital assets, has dropped over 21%

But within weeks the momentum shifted. As interest rate expectations shifted and tariff concerns with China lingered, Bitcoin prices declined. Additionally, as macroeconomic concerns resurfaced with a subdued labor market and questions about the state of the consumer economy, prices fell further, dropping to nearly 87,000 by mid-November.

More than a trillion dollars in notional value vanished across the market. Liquidations swept across leveraged positions, and the same cryptocurrency traders who had celebrated the rally as a sign of lasting structural change found themselves confronting once again the unforgiving nature of crypto’s sentiment-driven cycles.

The Limits of Political Sponsorship

These developments revived questions that lingered beneath the surface even during the most exuberant moments of the Trump era. Chief among them is whether political friendliness can truly substitute for the kind of regulatory clarity the industry has long avoided.

The administration’s embrace of crypto elevated the narrative and helped inspire a flood of new capital, yet it could not insulate the market from broader economic forces. Political warmth cannot change the mechanics of leverage, liquidity, or macro cycles.

The proposal for a national Bitcoin reserve remains emblematic of this tension. It captured imaginations and later generated executive directives, but its practical framework remains unresolved.

Agencies continue to study its implications, Congress has taken no decisive action, and the long-term consequences for the dollar and the global financial system remain unclear. The idea may continue to serve as a symbol of support, yet symbols alone cannot sustain a market that remains highly sensitive to economic stress.

Lessons of FTX and the Persistence of Old Patterns

The structural risks that came into view after the FTX collapse have not entirely disappeared. Courts delivered billions in restitution and judgments over the past year, marking an important step toward accountability. But the system that produced those vulnerabilities remains largely intact.

Leverage has not meaningfully declined. Perpetual futures still dominate exchange trading volumes. Open interest remains concentrated on a small group of exchanges that are themselves exposed to sharp swings in liquidity.

The rapid unwinding of positions during the latest correction demonstrated how easily a single shock can cascade across the ecosystem. While regulation has intensified in some areas, the underlying market structure continues to amplify volatility. The industry has moved away from the most obvious failings of the FTX era but has not yet built the guardrails needed to prevent similar dynamics.

The ETF Era and its Complications

The rise of Bitcoin exposure through ETFs has reshaped the investment landscape. These products have attracted institutions and have shifted trading from offshore venues to regulated platforms. This shift represents real progress compared with the environment that existed only a few years ago.

Still, several risks remain. Many of the most popular Bitcoin ETFs are structured as trusts or commodity pools rather than as registered investment companies. They provide familiarity and ease of access, yet they do not offer the full set of investor protections associated with 1940 Act funds.

These distinctions matter when markets become stressed. ETF flows can deepen liquidity but can also magnify volatility when sentiment reverses abruptly.

Taken together, these developments reveal a market that has matured in some respects while remaining vulnerable in others. The political environment is friendlier, institutional infrastructure has improved, and the industry now occupies a more secure place within the public financial imagination. Yet the behavior of prices over the past year indicates that crypto still relies heavily on momentum and the pursuit of extraordinary returns.

The very forces that drove Bitcoin’s ascent this year also enabled its sudden decline. As long as sentiment remains the primary engine of market direction, investors should expect cycles of exuberance and retrenchment to continue.

What Comes Next

It is tempting to view the past year as the moment when digital assets finally broke from their old cycles, yet the evidence points in a different direction. Rather than behaving like an emerging store of value or a counterweight to economic stress, crypto has increasingly moved in step with the rhythm of high beta equities.

Its rallies have been strongest when liquidity was abundant and risk sentiment was elevated, and its downturns have been most severe when macro conditions tightened. The pattern resembles the behavior of a speculative growth stock rather than an asset with defensive qualities, underscoring how far the market still is from achieving the stability its advocates often claim.

This alignment with broader risk markets helps explain why the industry’s structural vulnerabilities remain so visible. A more reliable pricing framework, one grounded in verifiable reserves, and transparent market structure, would go a long way toward reducing these pressures.

If matched with legitimate and recurring use cases that extend beyond trading and speculation, the resulting foundation could strengthen crypto’s claim to durability and soften the extreme swings that continue to define the asset class.

One year ago, we noted that the industry stood at a crossroads. That remains the case today, perhaps even more clearly, which is one of the structural reasons The Milwaukee Company, of which TMC Research is an affiliate, has refrained from allocating to digital assets.

While the Trump era has created openings for digital assets, it has also revealed that political support cannot counteract the force of economic cycles. The question now is whether the industry can mature into something more resilient or whether it will continue to behave like a high beta vehicle that rises quickly in good times and falls just as fast when the environment shifts.

The answer will depend on whether crypto can finally build the transparency, governance, and real world utility that would allow it to evolve from a speculative instrument into a lasting component of the financial system.

Original Source: The Milwaukee Company
